meta.note: Illustrative composite — rough estimates based on open datasets (The Pile, Dolma) and disclosed model training specs. No single model uses exactly this mix. Values represent approximate proportions by token count.

children.label: The Web
children.description: Web crawl data is the dominant ingredient in most large language models — CommonCrawl alone processes roughly 3 billion URLs per month. This category covers everything publicly indexable: news sites, personal blogs, corporate pages, how-to guides, recipe sites, opinion columns, and the vast middle of the internet that is neither expertly written nor entirely disposable.
bullets: CommonCrawl: petabyte-scale monthly web snapshots going back to 2008
Quality filters (C4, Gopher, OSCAR) discard ~90% of raw crawl data as spam, boilerplate, or near-duplicate
Web text introduces enormous vocabulary diversity but also misinformation, SEO gibberish, and temporal bias

children.label: Web Crawls
children.description: Raw CommonCrawl and derived filtered datasets (C4, Gopher, RefinedWeb) — the backbone of almost every large language model. Hundreds of billions of documents spanning the entire accessible internet.
bullets: C4 (Colossal Cleaned Crawl Corpus): 156GB of filtered English web text
RefinedWeb (Falcon): 5T tokens from CommonCrawl after aggressive deduplication
Dolma's dolmino-mix-1124 allocates 81% of its tokens to web sources

children.label: News Articles
children.description: RealNews, CC-News, and other curated news collections give models structured, factual prose: datelines, bylines, the inverted pyramid, and consistent temporal grounding. News writing is dense with proper nouns, organizations, places, and dates.
bullets: RealNews (Grover): ~32GB filtered from CommonCrawl, prioritizing credible news outlets
AP, Reuters, BBC: consistently crawled sources with high factual density
Creates temporal bias — events before the training cutoff are systematically over-represented
children.screenshotCaption: AP News — one of the most-indexed wire services in training corpora

children.label: Blogs & Personal Sites
children.description: Opinionated, personal, and niche writing — the long tail of the web. Technical blogs, personal essays, hobbyist sites, tutorial posts. Less formal than news, more focused than social media, and uniquely rich in first-person voice.
bullets: Technical writers like Simon Willison explain how systems work in meticulous, searchable prose
Substack and Ghost have added millions of newsletter-style essays since 2020
Captures informal registers, domain slang, and the 'I figured this out' voice
children.screenshotCaption: Simon Willison's weblog — canonical high-quality technical writing

children.label: Code & Math
children.description: Structured, formal, verifiable knowledge. Code has unambiguous semantics — it either runs or it doesn't. Mathematics has proofs. This is where models learn to reason precisely, to work through constraint satisfaction, and to follow chains of implication without drifting. The overrepresentation of code relative to its internet share is intentional: it punches above its weight in teaching formal thinking.
bullets: GitHub alone hosts 150M+ public repositories across hundreds of programming languages
Mathematical LaTeX source preserves symbolic structure that PDF rendering loses
Code is among the most deduplicated data — boilerplate and generated code is aggressively filtered

children.label: Code (GitHub)
children.description: Public repositories on GitHub, GitLab, and similar platforms — the world's largest archive of executable human reasoning. Source code in Python, JavaScript, C, Rust, SQL, Bash, and hundreds of other languages, plus the surrounding metadata: commit messages, README files, docstrings, and issue threads.
bullets: The Stack (BigCode): 3TB+ of permissively-licensed code filtered for quality and deduplication
Linux kernel: 30+ years, 30M+ lines, contributions from 25,000+ developers — one of the most-analyzed single artifacts
Commit messages and docstrings teach the relationship between code and its intent in natural language
children.screenshotCaption: Linux kernel on GitHub — 30+ years of collaborative programming

children.label: Stack Overflow
children.description: Structured Q&A where programmers ask specific questions and receive voted, curated answers. The format is almost a mini-proof: problem statement, failed attempt, correct solution, explanation of why it works. Error messages paired with fixes are among the most transferable training signals.
bullets: 58M+ questions spanning 170+ programming tags, going back to 2008
Voting surfaces the best explanations, creating a quality signal unavailable in raw crawl data
Error message → solution pairs teach debugging and error interpretation
children.screenshotCaption: Stack Overflow — the internet's most-voted programmer Q&A archive

children.label: arXiv Math/CS
children.description: Formal mathematical proofs and theoretical computer science, often in LaTeX source. This gives models the symbolic notation of mathematics — ∀, ∃, ⊢, → — along with proof techniques: induction, contradiction, construction, and the careful use of 'assume' and 'it follows that.'
bullets: arXiv hosts 2M+ papers in math, CS, physics, and quantitative biology
LaTeX source (not just PDFs) preserves equation structure and symbolic relationships
Proof techniques — induction, contradiction, construction — appear as recurring textual patterns
children.screenshotCaption: arXiv math paper — formal proofs with LaTeX source

children.label: MathOverflow
children.description: Expert-level mathematical discussion where PhD researchers ask and answer research-level questions. Among the densest concentrations of formal mathematical reasoning on the open internet — linking symbolic notation to natural-language explanation of why it works.
bullets: 95,000+ questions, predominantly research-level pure and applied mathematics
Experts explaining why proofs fail, the intuition behind theorems, edge cases
Unique for connecting formal notation with conversational mathematical explanation
children.screenshotCaption: MathOverflow — research-level mathematical Q&A

children.label: Academic Papers
children.description: Scholarly writing from peer-reviewed journals and preprint servers. Academic papers have a distinctive structure — abstract, methods, results, discussion, references — that teaches precise argumentation and hedged claims ('we hypothesize,' 'the data suggest'). The corpus includes everything from computer science to biomedicine to economics.
bullets: PubMed Central: 7M+ open-access biomedical full texts
arXiv: 2M+ preprints across STEM disciplines
Citation networks teach models the relationship between claims and evidence

children.label: AI/CS Papers
children.description: Computer science and AI preprints — including, recursively, papers about language models trained on data that includes papers about language models. The GPT-4 technical report is in the training data for models trained after it. This creates peculiar epistemic loops.
bullets: GPT-4 Technical Report (2023): trained-on and training-source simultaneously
Attention Is All You Need (2017): cited 100,000+ times, appears in countless forms in training data
Models trained on descriptions of their own architecture — a strange mirror
children.screenshotCaption: GPT-4 Technical Report on arXiv — a paper that became part of the training data for later models

children.label: Biomedical / PubMed
children.description: Open-access biomedical literature from PubMed Central — clinical trials, systematic reviews, case studies, molecular biology. Teaches models the grammar of scientific evidence: p-values, confidence intervals, study design, and careful causal hedging.
bullets: PubMed Central Open Access: 4M+ full-text biomedical papers freely available
Rich in structured data: dosages, diagnoses, genetic markers, outcome measures
Medical language includes Latin, Greek roots, and highly specialized abbreviations
children.screenshotCaption: PubMed Central — open-access biomedical literature

children.label: Other Disciplines
children.description: Social sciences, humanities, economics, physics, and everything else that makes it into open-access repositories. Diverse disciplinary vocabularies and writing conventions.
bullets: JSTOR Open Access: thousands of humanities and social science papers
SSRN: economics and legal studies preprints
Each discipline brings its own conventions for evidence and argument

children.label: Books
children.description: Long-form human thought — the most densely argued, most carefully edited form of written knowledge. Books teach models sustained reasoning across hundreds of pages, narrative coherence, chapter structure, and the thousand-year accumulation of ideas that constitute Western (and increasingly global) intellectual heritage. The legal status of this category is contested.
bullets: Project Gutenberg: 70,000+ public-domain works — everything before 1928
Books3 / LibGen derivatives: vast collections of copyrighted books scraped without authorization
Long-context coherence: books are the primary training signal for multi-paragraph reasoning

children.label: Public Domain
children.description: Project Gutenberg, Standard Ebooks, and other collections of works in the public domain — everything published before copyright applied or that has since expired. Austen, Dickens, Darwin, Twain, Du Bois, Wollstonecraft.
bullets: Project Gutenberg: 70,000+ works, all pre-1928 in the US or author-donated
Standard Ebooks: typographically careful, machine-readable reformatting of Gutenberg texts
Historical prose — Victorian sentences, 18th-century rhetoric — shapes the model's sense of 'formal writing'
children.screenshotCaption: Project Gutenberg — 70,000+ public domain books, freely available

children.label: Copyrighted Books
children.description: Books3, LibGen, and similar datasets derived from shadow libraries and pirated scans — millions of copyrighted books scraped and included without author consent. This is the most legally and ethically contested part of the training corpus, and the subject of ongoing litigation.
bullets: Books3 (The Pile): ~196,640 books scraped from Bibliotik, a private tracker
LibGen: ~4M books, used by several major training runs under contested 'fair use' arguments
Multiple lawsuits filed by authors (Authors Guild, individual writers) against AI companies
children.screenshotCaption: Internet Archive book digitization — a public-domain adjacent source; Books3 used more opaque piracy channels

children.label: Forums & Q&A
children.description: Informal discussion and community knowledge — the internet arguing, explaining, commiserating, and helping. Reddit, StackExchange, Hacker News. The tone ranges from thoughtful explanation to flame war, but at its best this category captures how humans actually reason through problems collaboratively and in real time.
bullets: Reddit: 800M+ posts and comments across 130,000+ active subreddits
StackExchange: 200+ Q&A sites beyond Stack Overflow, covering every domain
The 'explain like I'm five' register — lay explanations of complex topics — comes from here

children.label: Reddit (curated)
children.description: Filtered Reddit, keeping high-karma, high-engagement posts from substantive subreddits. The raw Reddit corpus is huge and low-quality; curated versions (Pushshift + quality filter) keep the 'explain it to me' discussions, in-depth analyses, and expert AMAs.
bullets: r/explainlikeimfive: lay explanations of complex topics — teaches the skill of analogy
r/AskScience, r/AskHistorians: expert-verified answers, a rare quality signal in forum data
Pushshift Reddit dataset: ~600GB of raw posts; most pipelines filter to <10% of that
children.screenshotCaption: r/explainlikeimfive — the 'explain it simply' register that models learned to mimic

children.label: StackExchange / Forums
children.description: The full StackExchange network beyond Stack Overflow: Math, Physics, English Language & Usage, Cooking, Travel, Law, and 200+ other communities. Each site has its own conventions for what a good answer looks like.
bullets: StackExchange data dump: all 200+ sites, fully public under CC-BY-SA
Math.SE, Physics.SE: semi-formal reasoning in natural language + LaTeX
English Language & Usage, Writing: teaches models about language itself
children.screenshotCaption: Stack Overflow — voted answers create a rare quality signal in forum data

children.label: Wikipedia
children.description: The encyclopedia that trained almost every language model that exists. Wikipedia is written in a distinctive neutral, encyclopedic register — 'X was born in Y and is known for Z' — that has influenced the default voice of AI more than any other single source. It is also available in 300+ languages.
bullets: English Wikipedia: 6.7M articles, 4.4B words — heavily upweighted in most training runs
Available in 300+ languages, making it the primary signal for multilingual models
The 'Wikipedia voice' — neutral, definitional, present-tense — is now the default AI register

children.label: English Wikipedia
children.description: 6.7 million articles covering everything from subatomic particles to obscure 19th-century politicians to every episode of The Simpsons. Heavily upweighted in most training runs because of its density, consistency, and citation discipline.
bullets: The Pile allocates ~17% to Wikipedia — dramatically more than its internet share
Infoboxes, citation templates, and category trees provide structured metadata
Talk pages and edit histories reveal the social construction of 'neutral' knowledge
children.screenshotCaption: English Wikipedia — the single most upweighted source in most training pipelines

children.label: Other Languages
children.description: Wikipedia in 300+ other languages — from Mandarin (1.4M articles) to Tamil (160,000+ articles) to Scots (60,000). These provide the multilingual foundation that allows models to operate in non-English contexts, though data quality and volume vary enormously by language.
bullets: Tamil Wikipedia: 160,000+ articles; Tamil has 80M+ speakers but far less web presence than its speaker count suggests
Low-resource languages are systematically underrepresented, even in Wikipedia
Multilingual Wikipedia teaches cross-lingual alignment — the same fact in different languages
children.screenshotCaption: Tamil Wikipedia — representing one of 300+ language editions included in training

children.label: Legal & Government
children.description: Court opinions, statutes, regulations, patents, and government documents. Legal writing has a distinctive structure: citations, precedent, numbered provisions, 'whereas' clauses, and very careful attention to the exact meaning of words. Government documents include everything from tax forms to Congressional records to administrative agency rulemakings.
bullets: FreeLaw / CourtListener: 7M+ federal and state court opinions
US Patent and Trademark Office: 11M+ patents going back to 1836
Federal Register: 85+ years of regulatory rulemaking in formal prose

children.label: Court Opinions
children.description: FreeLaw (CourtListener) and the Public Access to Court Electronic Records (PACER) system — millions of federal and state court opinions. Legal reasoning is extraordinarily precise about how language is used: a comma in a statute has been the subject of multimillion-dollar litigation.
bullets: CourtListener: 7M+ court documents, full text, from all federal circuits
Legal citations teach models formal reference structure: 347 U.S. 483 (1954)
Judicial writing teaches models argument from precedent and distinguishing cases
children.screenshotCaption: Justia — one of the public portals for freely-accessible US court opinions

children.label: Patents
children.description: Patents have a unique genre: independent claims, dependent claims, prior art, and specification sections. They teach models highly structured technical description with specific legal requirements about what must be disclosed and how broadly a claim may be drawn.
bullets: USPTO: 11M+ issued patents, full text searchable back to 1976
Patent claims require precise, exhaustive enumeration of every meaningful component
Prior art sections describe existing technology in controlled, citable form
children.screenshotCaption: Google Patents — a public interface for USPTO and international patent full text

children.label: Multilingual
children.description: Parallel corpora and multilingual text collections — documents that exist in multiple languages simultaneously. These are essential for training multilingual models because they provide alignment signal: the same meaning expressed in different linguistic forms. European Union documents are among the largest high-quality parallel corpora in the world.
bullets: Europarl: EU Parliament proceedings in 24 official languages, aligned sentence-by-sentence
CC-100: CommonCrawl filtered for 100 languages, unevenly quality-controlled
OPUS: aggregation of 90+ parallel translation corpora

children.label: Europarl / EU Docs
children.description: European Parliament proceedings translated into all 24 EU official languages, sentence-aligned. One of the highest-quality parallel corpora in existence: professional human translators, consistent subject matter, decades of coverage. Essential for multilingual model training.
bullets: 60M+ sentences in 24 languages, professionally translated by EU linguists
Sentence-aligned: the same speech in French, German, Polish, Bulgarian — simultaneously
Subject matter: law, regulation, debate — formal register across all languages
children.screenshotCaption: European Parliament — proceedings published in 24 official languages, professionally translated

children.label: CC-100 / Other
children.description: CommonCrawl filtered for 100 languages using language identification — CC-100 (Facebook AI Research) and similar multilingual web corpora. Coverage is uneven: high-resource languages like French and Spanish have billions of tokens; low-resource languages may have only millions.
bullets: CC-100: 2.5TB of filtered text across 100 languages, widely used for multilingual pretraining
Language distribution is highly unequal — English is 55%+ of indexed web content
Quality varies dramatically by language; some languages have mostly machine-translated content

children.label: Transcripts
children.description: Written records of spoken language — the closest training data gets to the oral register that humans default to in conversation. YouTube auto-captions, professional subtitles, podcast transcripts. This data teaches models how people actually talk: contractions, incomplete sentences, disfluencies, colloquialisms.
bullets: YouTube Data API: auto-generated captions for hundreds of millions of videos
OpenSubtitles: 3.7M subtitle files across 60+ languages, sourced from film and TV
Spoken language is structurally different from written — simpler syntax, more deixis ('this,' 'here,' 'now')

children.label: YouTube Captions
children.description: Auto-generated and manually-corrected captions from YouTube videos — lectures, tutorials, commentary, news, cooking shows, interviews. The auto-caption system has reached the point where the transcription errors themselves appear in training data.
bullets: YouTube hosts 800M+ videos; auto-captions are available for most English content
3Blue1Brown, Khan Academy, MIT OpenCourseWare: lecture content with visual explanations transcribed
YouTube transcripts collapse time — a 20-minute video becomes a 2,000-word document
children.screenshotCaption: YouTube — auto-captions from hundreds of millions of videos feed into transcript datasets

children.label: Subtitles
children.description: OpenSubtitles and similar movie/TV subtitle databases — the dialogue of cinema. This is conversational language at its most compressed and intentional: characters speaking in ways that carry story, reveal character, and advance plot.
bullets: OpenSubtitles: 3.7M files across 60+ languages, covering decades of film and television
Screenplay dialogue teaches the relationship between context and appropriate response
Available in dozens of languages with some alignment across language versions of the same film
children.screenshotCaption: OpenSubtitles — 3.7M subtitle files from film and television worldwide

children.label: Synthetic Data
children.description: Text generated by AI models to train other (or future versions of the same) AI models. Synthetic data is used primarily for pre-training augmentation in areas where human-generated data is scarce — especially formal mathematics and structured reasoning. Its use in pretraining is recent (2023+) and growing, though it constitutes a small share of current corpora.
bullets: rStar-Math: 747K math problems with step-by-step reasoning, generated by LLMs for LLM training
Phi-1, Phi-2, Phi-3 (Microsoft): deliberately trained on AI-generated 'textbook quality' text
Risk of 'model collapse': models trained on synthetic data derived from themselves may degrade over generations

children.label: Synthetic Reasoning
children.description: AI-generated reasoning traces and solutions for mathematics, logic, and coding — designed to teach models the step-by-step decomposition of problems. This is the fastest-growing new category in pre-training data.
bullets: rStar-Math (Microsoft): 747K math competition problems with Monte Carlo tree search-generated solutions
NuminaMath, DART-Math: augmented math datasets for competition-level problem solving
Recursive self-improvement: a model generates training data that trains a better version of itself
children.screenshotCaption: rStar-Math on arXiv — AI-generated mathematical reasoning traces used for training

children.label: Unknown
children.description: The part they don't tell you about. Most frontier model training runs include proprietary, licensed, or otherwise undisclosed data. The Foundation Model Transparency Index found that in 2025, the average transparency score for training data was 40 out of 100 — meaning most companies don't fully disclose what they trained on.
bullets: FMTI 2025: average transparency score 40/100 for training data disclosure
OpenAI, Google, Anthropic: none have fully disclosed their training data composition
Licensing deals with news publishers, content aggregators, and data brokers — amounts undisclosed

children.label: Proprietary / Undisclosed
children.description: We don't know what's in here. That's the point.
bullets: Licensing deals with undisclosed third parties
Internal proprietary datasets not subject to public disclosure
